video_title: The Grand Maester Conspiracy: what are the maesters up to?
claim: The maesters of the Citadel are running a secret, coordinated conspiracy against House Targaryen, dragons, and magic, manipulating politics and history to bring about their downfall.
sub_claims: A maester named Walys -- reportedly the son of an archmaester and a Hightower girl -- was involved in a plot with the Starks, Tullys, and possibly the Arryns and Baratheons to overthrow the Targaryens, helping cause Robert's Rebellion (per Lady Barbrey Dustin).

The maesters killed off the Targaryen dragons, directly or indirectly (e.g. by manipulating House Hightower into starting the Dance of the Dragons, or by poisoning/stunting the last dragons) (per Marwyn the Mage).
The maesters are conspiring to build a world without sorcery, prophecy, glass candles, or dragons -- a world without magic (per Marwyn the Mage).
asx_verdict: No hard evidence of an organized, realm-wide maester conspiracy exists, but it's very believable that at least some archmaesters at the Citadel individually distrust Targaryens, dragons, and magic, and might occasionally pull strings against them -- 'there's probably some sneaky shit going on, we just don't know how much.'
notes: Single video, one umbrella theory with two named in-world proponents treated as sub-claims/variants: Lady Barbrey Dustin (Robert's Rebellion angle, sub_claim 0) and Marwyn the Mage (Dance of the Dragons + anti-magic angle, sub_claims 1-2). Video also tests the theory against two individual maesters (Pycelle, Cressen) as case studies. Sourced from the main books, TWOIAF/World app, and the Dance of the Dragons novellas (tagged fab); no show content.

paraphrase: Barbrey Dustin, Lady of Barrowton, deeply distrusts the maesters, calling them 'grey rats.'
book_hint: ADWD, Barbrey Dustin dialogue (Theon/Reek chapters, Barrowton)

paraphrase: Dustin points out maesters often read and write letters for their lords, so they could possibly twist the words for their own ends.
book_hint: ADWD, Barbrey Dustin dialogue
spoken_quote: the words for their own ends

paraphrase: Because maesters give up their family names, Dustin says you can't know who they truly are or where they come from, or whose interests they serve.
book_hint: ADWD, Barbrey Dustin dialogue

paraphrase: Dustin says lords make maesters privy to all their shames and secrets, a part of every council, and before too long the ruler has become the ruled.
book_hint: ADWD, Barbrey Dustin dialogue
spoken_quote: make them privy to all our shames and secrets, a part of every council. And before too long, the ruler has become the ruled

paraphrase: Maesters write the history books, educate young lordlings, count the votes at councils, and whisper into the ears of the most powerful people in Westeros, giving them broad means to influence politics if they chose to.

Dustin claims the maesters were closely involved in a plot against the Targaryens before Robert's Rebellion, with the Starks, Tullys, and maybe the Arryns and Baratheons plotting rebellion long before the war began, led in part by a maester named Walys, said to be the son of an archmaester of the Citadel and a 'Hightower girl' -- House Hightower being one of the biggest supporters of the maesters.
book_hint: ADWD, Barbrey Dustin dialogue

paraphrase: Marwyn the Mage is an unconventional archmaester who keeps company with whores and hedge wizards, sacrifices to 'queer gods,' spent years in the east studying with warlocks and shadowbinders and searching for lost books, taught Mirri Maz Duur to heal, and wears a Valyrian steel mask marking him an expert on magic -- lending his claims some weight.
book_hint: AFFC, Samwell's Citadel chapters, Marwyn introduction

paraphrase: At the end of Feast, Marwyn tells Sam that the maesters are conspiring to build a world without sorcery, prophecy, glass candles, or dragons.
book_hint: AFFC, Samwell's final chapter, Marwyn dialogue
spoken_quote: sorcery or prophecy or glass candles

paraphrase: Marwyn implies that the maesters killed all the dragons the last time around.
book_hint: AFFC, Samwell's final chapter, Marwyn dialogue
spoken_quote: killed all the dragons the last time around

paraphrase: Marwyn implies the maesters didn't trust Maester Aemon because he was a Targaryen.
book_hint: AFFC, Samwell's final chapter, Marwyn dialogue

paraphrase: Marwyn implies the maesters are willing to kill to further their goals.
book_hint: AFFC, Samwell's final chapter, Marwyn dialogue

paraphrase: As the historians of Westeros, the maesters would be painfully aware of the damage done to the realm by the Targaryens and their dragons through war, misrule, and madness; several Grand Maesters were also killed by Targaryen kings, giving the maesters plausible motive to oppose the dynasty.
book_hint: Citadel/Targaryen history

paraphrase: Maesters don't trust magic because it's wild, dangerous, and hard to control or understand, which is a real challenge to the maesters' power as scholars and advisors.
When Aemon talks about serving on the Wall instead of becoming an archmaester, it sounds like it was his own choice, not an exile.
book_hint: ASOS, Jon POV, Aemon dialogue

paraphrase: The official World of Ice and Fire app says Aemon went to the Wall so he couldn't be used politically against his brother Egg, the king -- not because the Citadel distrusted him as a Targaryen.
book_hint: World of Ice and Fire companion app, Aemon Targaryen entry

paraphrase: At least one Targaryen did serve as an archmaester at the Citadel, though admittedly a long time ago, undercutting the idea the Citadel systematically distrusts Targaryens.
book_hint: Citadel history

paraphrase: The 'last time around' Marwyn refers to was about 170 years ago, when the Targaryens had 21 dragons; most were killed during the Dance of the Dragons civil war -- 12 killed in battle, 5 killed by a rioting mob of commoners, 2 flew off, and 1 vanished, leaving one young dragon (Morning) alive under Targaryen control -- i.e. the dragons died in a war, not by direct maester action.
book_hint: Fire & Blood / Dance of the Dragons novellas

paraphrase: It has been suggested the last dragon hatchlings, which were stunted and small, might have been poisoned.

The maesters' official explanation for the dragons no longer growing large is that the Targaryens kept them in the King's Landing Dragonpit enclosure -- but Daenerys's dragons still seem to grow fine while kept chained up, casting doubt on that explanation.
book_hint: ADWD, Daenerys POV, dragons chained in Meereen

paraphrase: In the Princess and the Queen and Rogue Prince novellas and The World of Ice and Fire, it doesn't look like the maesters were involved in starting the Dance of the Dragons -- Grand Maester Orwyle and Grand Maester Runciter both try to prevent the war from starting.
book_hint: Princess and the Queen / Rogue Prince novellas; TWOIAF Dance of the Dragons chapter

paraphrase: Maester Norren helps a dragon and its rider flee the Dance of the Dragons war.
book_hint: Princess and the Queen / Rogue Prince novellas

paraphrase: If you want to get paranoid, these in-universe accounts of the Dance are written by maesters, who may have covered up their own involvement in the war.
A septon and a court jester also contributed to these accounts of the Dance, making a maester cover-up seem less likely.

The Dance of the Dragons was in large part caused by House Hightower -- Queen Alicent Hightower wanted her son Aegon to inherit over King Viserys's chosen heir Rhaenyra -- and House Hightower is the same house Lady Dustin connects to the maester (Walys) who helped cause Robert's Rebellion, and the same house known to closely support the maesters.
book_hint: Fire & Blood, origins of the Dance of the Dragons

paraphrase: It doesn't seem likely the Hightowers were conspiring with the maesters against the Targaryens, because the Hightowers have actually been very loyal to the Targaryens.
book_hint: Fire & Blood / House Hightower history

paraphrase: It might be that the maesters manipulated the Hightowers into starting the Dance of the Dragons, in the same way the maesters apparently manipulated the Starks into rebelling against the Targaryens.

Daenerys says magic had died in the west when the Doom fell on Valyria; since the Doom happened centuries ago and far away, the maesters probably weren't involved in magic's original decline (though the Faceless Men might have been).
book_hint: Daenerys POV chapters
spoken_quote: Magic had died in the west when the Doom fell on Valyria

paraphrase: Maester Luwin says magic does not work.
book_hint: AGOT/ACOK, Bran POV, Maester Luwin dialogue

paraphrase: Maester Luwin says perhaps magic was once a mighty force in the world, but no longer -- a view shared by most other maesters.
book_hint: AGOT/ACOK, Bran POV, Maester Luwin dialogue
spoken_quote: Perhaps magic was once a mighty force in the world, but no longer

paraphrase: Part of a maester's Citadel training involves spending a night in a darkened room with an obsidian/dragonglass 'glass candle'; lighting it requires magic, and the whole point of the exercise is to show maesters that magic doesn't work -- raising the question of what sense a conspiracy against magic makes if maesters believe there is no magic anymore.
book_hint: AFFC, Samwell's Citadel chapters

paraphrase: Quaithe and Marwyn have both realized that glass candles are burning again -- magic is returning, with new dragons born, the Others returned, Melisandre's magic growing stronger, and resurrections happening.
book_hint: Quaithe/Marwyn references across ACOK-ADWD

paraphrase: Leo Tyrell says old powers waken, shadows stir, and an age of wonder and terror -- an age for gods and heroes -- will soon be upon us, framed as something the maesters would not want to happen.
spoken_quote: Old powers waken. Shadows stir. An age of wonder and terror will soon be upon us, an age for gods and heroes

paraphrase: The maesters aren't secretive about magic -- they openly discuss it in their books and have an official Conclave seat for an archmaester who's an expert on magic -- so it's unclear how a conspiracy against magic would even work.
In Book 1, when the small council discusses what to do about Daenerys, Pycelle advocates assassinating her -- though most of the rest of the council does too, so it might not mean much on its own.
book_hint: AGOT, Eddard POV, small council chapter

paraphrase: During Robert's Rebellion, Pycelle convinced the Mad King to open the gates of King's Landing to Tywin Lannister's army, leading to the Sack of King's Landing, the death of King Aerys, and the end of the line of Targaryen kings.
book_hint: Robert's Rebellion background lore / Sack of King's Landing

paraphrase: Under sharp interrogation by Tyrion and then Cersei, Pycelle breaks down weeping and reveals his true motivation was not a conspiracy against the Targaryens but a massive man-crush on Tywin Lannister -- he says he's Tywin's man, hoped Tywin would become king, and that all he did, he did for House Lannister.
book_hint: ACOK/ASOS, Tyrion and Cersei POV interrogation scenes

paraphrase: In The World of Ice and Fire, Pycelle says the gods made and shaped Tywin to rule.
book_hint: The World of Ice and Fire, Pycelle quote

paraphrase: Pycelle goes on about 'the man beneath the armor,' referring to Tywin.
book_hint: The World of Ice and Fire, Pycelle quote

paraphrase: It was loyalty to Lannister, not a conspiracy against Targaryens, that led Pycelle to bring about the Mad King's downfall.
You could claim Pycelle's doddering-old-man act is actually a master of deception hiding true involvement in a conspiracy -- he couldn't be ruled out unless you could see what he was really thinking.

Maester Cressen, who serves Stannis at Dragonstone, tries to kill the 'red witch' Melisandre in the Prologue of Clash of Kings -- which looks exactly like something someone in a conspiracy against magic would do.
book_hint: ACOK Prologue, Cressen POV

paraphrase: The Prologue is written from Cressen's POV, showing his thoughts; a sort of ideological battle between Melisandre's magic and Cressen's denial of it is part of his motivation.
book_hint: ACOK Prologue, Cressen POV

paraphrase: Cressen's main motivation is that he wants to protect Stannis, whom he loves and thinks of as the son he never had.
book_hint: ACOK Prologue, Cressen POV

paraphrase: Cressen feels Melisandre is leading Stannis astray by convincing him to kill his brother and to sail to war without having allies.
book_hint: ACOK Prologue, Cressen POV

paraphrase: Cressen's real reason for trying to kill Melisandre is fatherly love for Stannis, not a maester conspiracy against magic.
book_hint: ACOK Prologue, Cressen POV

paraphrase: Marwyn's claims are probably exaggerated -- he might have an ulterior motive for scaring Sam off from telling the Citadel about Daenerys.

Many characters are converging on the Citadel: Sam Tarly has been sent to study and earn his maester's chain, Jaqen H'ghar has come in disguise and acquired a key said to unlock every door in the Citadel, Sarella Martell (Oberyn's bastard daughter) has come in disguise as the acolyte Alleras, and Euron Greyjoy's ironborn have started raiding around Oldtown -- suggesting more about the maesters will be revealed.
book_hint: AFFC/ADWD, Samwell POV + broader Citadel setup
